{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.6": 0.5}}

Summary: critique flagging a major mismatch between the described study and the claimed contribution, plus several supporting issues.

**Main problem: the contribution claim does not match the study described.**

The closing sentence claims the contribution is "a deployment-validated automated turn-cue system that quantitatively improves team satisfaction." But nothing in the preceding paragraphs describes building, deploying, or evaluating such a system:

- The instrumentation is described as a "meeting-analysis tool that captured speaker turns, interruptions, and silence intervals" — i.e., passive measurement, not cue delivery.
- The analysis (HMM over turn sequences, regression on satisfaction) is observational, not an intervention study.
- There is no mention of which teams received automated cues, how cues were delivered, or any A/B/control assignment.

So the contribution sentence overclaims on at least three axes: (a) that a system was built, (b) that it was automated, and (c) that the study established a causal/quantitative improvement attributable to it.

**Related evidence-claim gap: the "23% reduction / 0.6 points higher" result.**

The results paragraph compares "teams using a structured turn-taking protocol" against "teams without the protocol." But the methods paragraph never establishes that such a protocol was assigned, manipulated, or even that the 24 teams split into protocol vs. non-protocol groups. As written, the comparison appears out of nowhere. Either:
- the study was actually a quasi-experiment (some teams adopted a protocol, others did not) — in which case the design, group sizes, and assignment mechanism need to be stated; or
- "structured turn-taking protocol" is being inferred post hoc from the HMM discourse states — in which case "teams using" vs. "teams without" is misleading framing for what is really a correlational contrast.

This needs to be resolved before the numbers can be trusted.

**Novelty framing is unclear.**

The opening positions prior work as already having shown that turn-taking cues reduce miscommunication, that the effect generalizes multimodally, and that it scales to 12-person teams via automated cue presentation (Chen et al.). The closing then says the present effect sizes "match prior controlled-study findings." If the result merely replicates prior controlled findings in the field, the delta over Chen et al. in particular needs to be articulated — is it the field setting, the longer duration, the engineering-team population, the HMM-based discourse-state analysis, or the satisfaction outcome? Right now the reader cannot tell what is new.

**Smaller issues.**

- Citations are by author name only with no years; at minimum add years, and verify these references exist as cited (I cannot verify Wallace et al., Park & Liu, or Chen et al. from the text alone).
- The 32% figure from Wallace et al. is given without a baseline or outcome definition, which makes the later "matches prior findings" claim hard to evaluate.
- "Approximately 480 hours" with 24 teams over four weeks works out to ~5 hours/team/week of recorded meetings — worth stating explicitly so readers can assess density.
- The HMM and regression are mentioned in one sentence without specifying the number of states, covariates, or how multiple comparisons across 24 teams × 4 weeks were handled.

**Recommended revisions, in priority order:**

1. Reconcile the contribution sentence with what was actually done. Either reframe the contribution as an observational field characterization of turn-taking and satisfaction, or add the missing description of the deployed cue system and its evaluation design.
2. Clarify the study design behind the protocol-vs-no-protocol comparison (assignment, group sizes, confounds).
3. State the specific delta over Chen et al. and the other cited work.
4. Add years to citations and a one-line operationalization for the 32% prior result.

Uncertainty: I cannot verify the cited references or the reported statistics; my critique assumes the artifact is the only available description of the study.
